words.meaning_en: to close or secure something firmly
words.origin_en: Comes from Old English 'fæstnian', meaning 'to make firm'.
words.origin_es: Proviene del inglés antiguo 'fæstnian', que significa 'hacer firme'.
examples: Fasten your seatbelt. 🔒
Please fasten the buttons. 👕

words.meaning_en: not noticed; ignored unintentionally
words.origin_en: From 'over' + 'look', originally meaning 'to supervise from above'.
words.origin_es: De 'over' + 'look', originalmente significaba 'supervisar desde arriba'.
examples: The detail was overlooked. 🙈
I overlooked the upper body movement. 👤

words.meaning_en: formally ended or stopped a practice
words.origin_en: From Latin 'abolere', meaning 'destroy'.
words.origin_es: Del latín 'abolere', que significa 'destruir'.
examples: Capital punishment was abolished. ⚖️
Slavery was abolished. ⛓️

words.meaning_en: the legal killing of someone as punishment
words.origin_en: From Latin 'capitale' (regarding the head).
words.origin_es: Del latín 'capitale', relacionado con la cabeza (decapitación).
examples: Many countries have abolished capital punishment. ⚖️

words.meaning_en: a long, narrow strip of fabric
words.origin_en: Possibly from Old French 'ruban'.
words.origin_es: Posiblemente del francés antiguo 'ruban'.
examples: She tied a ribbon on her hair. 🎀

words.meaning_en: delays progress; also an offensive insult
words.origin_en: From Latin 'retardare' (to slow).
words.origin_es: Del latín 'retardare' (ralentizar).
examples: Traffic retards progress. 🚗🐌

words.meaning_en: cutting away small pieces
words.origin_en: From Old English 'trymman' (to arrange).
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'trymman' (organizar).
examples: I am trimming the plants. ✂️🌿

words.meaning_en: a field of grass and flowers
words.origin_en: From Old English 'mæd'.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'mæd'.
examples: We walked through the meadow. 🌼

words.meaning_en: informal negative contraction for am not / is not / are not
words.meaning_es: contracción informal de am not / is not / are not
words.origin_en: Appeared in the 1700s as 'an't' meaning 'am not'.
words.origin_es: Apareció en 1700s como 'an't' que significaba 'am not'.
examples: I ain’t going. 🚫
This ain’t right. ❌

words.meaning_en: your parent's sister
words.origin_en: From Latin 'amita'.
words.origin_es: Del latín 'amita'.
examples: My aunt is visiting today. 👩‍🦰

words.meaning_en: a ceremonial or patriotic song
words.origin_en: Old English 'antefn' meaning a hymn.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'antefn', que significa himno.
examples: The national anthem started playing. 🇪🇨🎵

words.meaning_en: extremely hot
words.origin_en: From Old French 'boillir'.
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'boillir'.
examples: It’s boiling outside. 🥵🔥

words.meaning_en: a bird’s home
words.origin_en: From Old English 'nest'.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'nest'.
examples: The bird built a nest. 🐦

words.meaning_en: deep sadness
words.origin_en: From Old English 'sorh'.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'sorh'.
examples: He felt great sorrow. 😢

words.meaning_en: fellow members of a group, often in military or political contexts
words.origin_en: From French 'camarade', ultimately from Spanish 'camarada' (roommate).
words.origin_es: Del francés 'camarade', originalmente del español 'camarada' (compañero de habitación).
examples: The soldiers fought bravely alongside their comrades.

words.meaning_en: to give something and receive something in return
words.origin_en: From Old French 'eschangier', meaning 'to exchange'.
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'eschangier', que significa 'intercambiar'.
examples: They exchanged gifts during the celebration.

words.meaning_en: to accept, admit, or recognize something
words.origin_en: From Middle English, from 'knowledge' with prefix 'ac-'.
words.origin_es: Del inglés medio, de 'knowledge' con prefijo 'ac-'.
examples: She acknowledged her mistake and apologized.

words.meaning_en: improved or made better
words.origin_en: From Old French 'enhaucier', meaning 'to raise'.
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'enhaucier', que significa 'elevar'.
examples: The enhanced features of the app improved user experience.
She enhanced the quality of her project by adding visuals.

words.meaning_en: to use something to its maximum advantage
words.origin_en: From 'leverage', which comes from 'lever' (a tool for mechanical advantage).
words.origin_es: De 'leverage', que viene de 'lever' (palanca para ventaja mecánica).
examples: The company is leveraging its resources to expand globally.
He is leveraging his skills to secure a better position.

words.meaning_en: a thick, sweet liquid made from sugar, used in cooking or as a topping
words.origin_en: From Arabic 'sharab' (beverage) via Medieval Latin.
words.origin_es: Del árabe 'sharab' (bebida) vía latín medieval.
examples: She poured syrup over the pancakes.
The doctor prescribed cough syrup for her cold.

words.meaning_en: a type of tree known for its leaves and sweet sap used to make syrup
words.origin_en: From Old English 'mapel'.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'mapel'.
examples: The maple tree is famous for its vibrant autumn colors.
Maple syrup is a popular topping for pancakes.

words.meaning_en: more courageous, confident, or striking
words.meaning_es: más audaz, más atrevido, más destacado
words.origin_en: Comparative form of 'bold', from Old English 'bald' (brave).
words.origin_es: Forma comparativa de 'bold', del inglés antiguo 'bald' (valiente).
examples: She became bolder after facing her fears.
The design was made bolder with vibrant colors.

words.meaning_en: the process of careful thought or discussion before making a decision
words.origin_en: From Latin 'deliberatio', from 'deliberare' (to consider carefully).
words.origin_es: Del latín 'deliberatio', de 'deliberare' (considerar cuidadosamente).
examples: After much deliberation, she decided to take the job.
The committee's deliberation lasted for several hours.

words.meaning_en: moving closer to something in distance, time, or situation
words.origin_en: From Old French 'approchier', from Latin 'appropiare'.
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'approchier', del latín 'appropiare'.
examples: The deadline is quickly approaching.
The car is approaching the intersection.
She is approaching the problem with a new perspective.

words.meaning_en: a spice made from the dried bark of certain tropical trees, commonly used in cooking and baking
words.origin_en: From Greek 'kinnamomon', ultimately from Hebrew 'qinnamon'.
words.origin_es: Del griego 'kinnamomon', originalmente del hebreo 'qinnamon'.
examples: She added a pinch of cinnamon to the apple pie.
Cinnamon is often used to flavor teas and coffee.

words.meaning_en: a legal document or justification for an action
words.origin_en: From Old French 'warant', of Germanic origin.
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'warant', de origen germánico.
examples: The judge issued a warrant for his arrest.
This action warrants further investigation.
Her achievements warrant recognition.

words.meaning_en: the past tense of 'undergo', meaning to experience or endure something
words.origin_en: Past tense of 'undergo', from Old English 'undergan'.
words.origin_es: Pasado de 'undergo', del inglés antiguo 'undergan'.
examples: She underwent surgery last week.
The company underwent major changes.
He underwent a difficult training program.

words.meaning_en: from the beginning to the end of something, or across an entire area
words.meaning_es: a lo largo de, durante todo, en todo
words.origin_en: From 'through' + 'out', Middle English.
words.origin_es: De 'through' + 'out', inglés medio.
examples: He worked hard throughout the year.
The news spread throughout the country.
Throughout the course, we will study different topics.

words.meaning_en: to have a deep emotional impact, to be understood, or to produce sound vibrations
words.origin_en: From Latin 'resonare', meaning 'to resound'.
words.origin_es: Del latín 'resonare', que significa 'resonar'.
examples: Her speech truly resonates with me.
The book resonates with readers of all ages.
The sound of the bell resonates through the hall.

words.meaning_en: to give support, confidence, or motivation to someone
words.origin_en: From Old French 'encoragier', from 'corage' (courage).
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'encoragier', de 'corage' (coraje).
examples: Parents should encourage their children to read.
Her words encouraged me to keep going.
The company encourages innovation and creativity.

words.meaning_en: used to contrast or introduce a different idea
words.origin_en: From 'how' + 'ever', Middle English.
words.origin_es: De 'how' + 'ever', inglés medio.
examples: She is very talented. However, she lacks experience.
The weather was bad; however, we still went outside.

words.meaning_en: structures for storing goods or isolated systems within an organization
words.origin_en: From Spanish 'silo', ultimately from Greek 'siros' (pit for grain).
words.origin_es: Del español 'silo', originalmente del griego 'siros' (foso para grano).
examples: Farmers store grain in silos to keep it dry.
Departments working in silos reduce efficiency.
We need to break down data silos for better collaboration.

words.meaning_en: honest, open, or direct about something; also payment in advance
words.origin_en: From 'up' + 'front', American English.
words.origin_es: De 'up' + 'front', inglés estadounidense.
examples: We need the payment upfront before starting the work.
I'll be upfront, I don't think this plan will work.
She was upfront about the challenges of the project.

words.meaning_en: expresses a feeling or desire for something to happen
words.origin_en: From 'hopeful' + '-ly'.
words.origin_es: De 'hopeful' + '-ly'.
examples: Hopefully, it won't rain tomorrow.
Hopefully, we can solve this issue soon.
Hopefully, the next season will be better.

words.meaning_en: causing great pleasure or joy
words.origin_en: From 'delight' + '-ful', from Latin 'delectare'.
words.origin_es: De 'delight' + '-ful', del latín 'delectare'.
examples: We had a delightful evening at the park.
She's a delightful person to work with.
This cake is absolutely delightful.

words.meaning_en: eager or enthusiastic; also sharp or acute
words.origin_en: From Old English 'cene' (brave, bold).
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'cene' (valiente, audaz).
examples: She was keen to start her new job.
He has a keen eye for detail.

words.meaning_en: appealing or attractive
words.origin_en: From Latin 'temptare' (to test, try).
words.origin_es: Del latín 'temptare' (probar, intentar).
examples: The cake looked so tempting I had to try it.

words.meaning_en: a person captured and held against their will
words.origin_en: From Old French 'hostage', from 'hoste' (guest, host).
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'hostage', de 'hoste' (huésped, anfitrión).
examples: The hostage was released after negotiations.

words.meaning_en: relating to a person of white race
words.origin_en: From 'Caucasus', a region between Europe and Asia.
words.origin_es: De 'Cáucaso', región entre Europa y Asia.
examples: He is of Caucasian descent.

words.meaning_en: an unusual or eccentric person
words.origin_en: Origin uncertain, possibly from Old English 'frician' (to dance).
words.origin_es: Origen incierto, posiblemente del inglés antiguo 'frician' (bailar).
examples: He's a total freak when it comes to gaming.

words.meaning_en: small portions of something
words.origin_en: From Old English 'bita' (piece bitten off).
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'bita' (pedazo mordido).
examples: I only need a few bits of information to complete the task.

words.meaning_en: extending in one direction without curving; honest and direct; heterosexual
words.origin_en: From Old English 'streht', past participle of 'streccan' (to stretch).
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'streht', participio pasado de 'streccan' (estirar).
examples: Draw a straight line from A to B.
Go straight ahead for two blocks.
I'll give you a straight answer: I don't know.
Let me get this straight - you want me to do what?
She has straight hair.
He's been working for 10 hours straight (continuously).

words.meaning_en: the first appearance of light in the morning; the beginning of something
words.meaning_es: amanecer, alba; el comienzo de algo
words.origin_en: From Old English 'dagian' (to become day), related to 'day'.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'dagian' (convertirse en día), relacionado con 'day' (día).
examples: We woke up at dawn to watch the sunrise.
The birds start singing at dawn.
It suddenly dawned on me that I forgot my keys.
This marks the dawn of a new era.
Dawn is breaking over the mountains.

words.meaning_en: the edge of a sidewalk; to restrain or control something
words.origin_en: From Latin 'curvare' (to bend, curve). Originally referred to a curved restraining strap, later applied to the curved edge of a street.
words.origin_es: Del latín 'curvare' (doblar, curvar). Originalmente se refería a una correa de restricción curva, luego aplicado al borde curvo de la calle.
examples: The car hit the curb and got a flat tire.
Don't park too close to the curb.
We need to curb our spending this month.
The government is trying to curb pollution.
She tried to curb her anger during the meeting.
Curb your enthusiasm! (control your excitement)

words.meaning_en: a rope or chain used to control a dog or other animal; to control or restrain
words.origin_en: From Old French 'laisse', meaning 'a thong, string, or lace'.
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'laisse', que significa 'una correa, cuerda o cordón'.
examples: Keep your dog on a leash in the park.
She walked her dog on a short leash.
The boss keeps him on a tight leash. (controls him closely)
He needs to be kept on a leash. (needs to be controlled)
The new policy gives employees a longer leash. (more freedom)
Leash your dog before entering the store.

words.meaning_en: a physical fight using only fists; a brawl or scuffle
words.meaning_es: pelea a puñetazos, pelea con los puños
words.origin_en: Compound word from 'fist' (closed hand) + 'fight' (combat). Used since the 1600s to describe hand-to-hand combat without weapons.
words.origin_es: Palabra compuesta de 'fist' (puño) + 'fight' (pelea). Usado desde los 1600s para describir combate cuerpo a cuerpo sin armas.
examples: The argument escalated into a fistfight.
Two men got into a fistfight outside the bar.
The referee stopped the fistfight before anyone got seriously hurt.
A fistfight broke out in the parking lot.
He walked away to avoid a fistfight.

words.meaning_en: plural of fist; closed hands with fingers curled into the palm
words.meaning_es: puños; manos cerradas con los dedos doblados hacia la palma
words.origin_en: From Old English 'fȳst', meaning 'clenched hand'. Related to Old German 'fūst'.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'fȳst', que significa 'mano cerrada'. Relacionado con el alemán antiguo 'fūst'.
examples: He clenched his fists in anger.
She raised her fists in victory.
The boxer held up his fists ready to fight.
He pounded his fists on the table.
They shook their fists at the referee.
She punched the air with both fists.

words.meaning_en: used to emphasize a statement or response; in truth; truly
words.origin_en: From Middle English 'in dede', literally meaning 'in fact' or 'in action'. Combines 'in' + 'deed' (action).
words.origin_es: Del inglés medio 'in dede', literalmente 'en hecho' o 'en acción'. Combina 'in' + 'deed' (acción).
examples: That is indeed a good idea.
Indeed, I agree with you completely.
The view was indeed spectacular.
It was indeed a challenging task.
Thank you very much indeed!

words.meaning_en: hitting your toe or foot against something hard; in testing, creating fake objects to simulate behavior
words.meaning_es: golpear el dedo del pie contra algo duro; en pruebas, crear objetos falsos para simular comportamiento
words.origin_en: From 'stub', Middle English 'stubbe', meaning a tree stump or short projecting part.
words.origin_es: De 'stub', del inglés medio 'stubbe', que significa tocón de árbol o parte corta que sobresale.
examples: I keep stubbing my toe on the table leg.
She was stubbing her foot against the corner.
Stubbing your toe really hurts!
We're stubbing the API calls in our unit tests.
Stubbing dependencies helps isolate the code being tested.

words.meaning_en: a passage between rows of seats or shelves
words.meaning_es: pasillo entre filas de asientos o estantes
words.origin_en: From Old French 'ele' meaning 'wing' (of a church). Note: sounds exactly like 'isle' (island) but spelled differently.
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'ele' que significa 'ala' (de una iglesia). Nota: suena exactamente como 'isle' (isla) pero se escribe diferente.
examples: The bride walked down the aisle.
You'll find the cereal in aisle 5.
Please keep the aisle clear.
I prefer an aisle seat on the plane.
He was blocking the aisle with his cart.

words.meaning_en: a small piece or brief extract of something; in programming, a reusable piece of code
words.meaning_es: fragmento, pedazo pequeño; en programación, trozo de código reutilizable
words.origin_en: From 'snip' (to cut with scissors) + diminutive suffix '-et'. First recorded in the 1660s.
words.origin_es: De 'snip' (cortar con tijeras) + sufijo diminutivo '-et'. Registrado por primera vez en los 1660s.
examples: I heard a snippet of their conversation.
The article included snippets from the interview.
Can you show me a snippet of the code?
She shared a snippet of the song on social media.
I only caught snippets of information from the meeting.
Use this code snippet to solve the problem.

words.meaning_en: mixed up in a confused or disorderly way; cooked eggs stirred while frying; rushed or moved quickly
words.meaning_es: revuelto, mezclado de forma desordenada; huevos revueltos; moverse apresuradamente
words.origin_en: From 'scramble', possibly imitative or from Dutch 'schrammen' (to scratch). Used since the 1500s.
words.origin_es: De 'scramble', posiblemente imitativo o del holandés 'schrammen' (rascar). Usado desde los 1500s.
examples: I had scrambled eggs for breakfast.
The letters were all scrambled up.
He scrambled the password to make it more secure.
The words in the puzzle are scrambled.
They scrambled to finish the project on time.
The fighter jets scrambled in response to the threat.

words.meaning_en: an extended period or duration; 'long' here means extended in duration, not physical length
words.meaning_es: mucho tiempo, largo tiempo; 'long' aquí significa extendido en duración, no longitud física
words.origin_en: From Old English 'lang' meaning 'having great linear extent'. Extended metaphorically to time duration since Old English period.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'lang' que significa 'tener gran extensión lineal'. Extendido metafóricamente a la duración del tiempo desde el período del inglés antiguo.
examples: It's been a long time since we last met.
I haven't seen you in a long time!
That took a long time to finish.
We've been waiting for a long time.
Long time no see! (informal greeting)
It will take a long time to complete this project.
words.note: ⚠️ IMPORTANT: 'Long' with TIME means DURATION (how much time), NOT distance. We use 'long' for time periods: 'a long time', 'how long?', 'for a long while'. We DON'T say 'a big time' or 'a large time'. Compare: 'a long road' (physical distance) vs 'a long time' (duration). Other time expressions with 'long': 'all day long', 'all night long', 'how long will it take?', 'as long as needed'.

words.meaning_en: making a deep, continuous sound; stomach making noise from hunger; early signs of discontent or unrest
words.meaning_es: hacer un sonido profundo y continuo; estómago haciendo ruido por hambre; señales tempranas de descontento o inquietud
words.origin_en: From 'rumble', Middle English, possibly imitative of low, rolling sounds like thunder. First recorded in the 1300s.
words.origin_es: De 'rumble', inglés medio, posiblemente imitativo de sonidos bajos y rodantes como el trueno. Registrado por primera vez en los 1300s.
examples: I heard a rumbling sound in the distance.
The thunder was rumbling across the sky.
My stomach is rumbling—I'm hungry!
There were rumblings of discontent among the employees.
The truck went rumbling down the street.
We heard rumblings about possible changes in management.

words.meaning_en: the lower part of the leg; a cut of meat from the leg; a makeshift knife or stabbing weapon (prison slang); the shaft or straight part of a tool
words.meaning_es: la parte inferior de la pierna; un corte de carne de la pierna; un cuchillo improvisado o arma punzante (jerga carcelaria); el eje o parte recta de una herramienta
words.origin_en: From Old English 'sceanca', meaning 'leg bone' or 'shin bone'. Related to German 'Schenkel'.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'sceanca', que significa 'hueso de la pierna' o 'espinilla'. Relacionado con el alemán 'Schenkel'.
examples: The recipe calls for lamb shank.
He was shanked in prison. (stabbed with an improvised weapon)
The shank of the drill bit was too short.
She ordered the braised beef shank for dinner.
The prisoner made a shank from a piece of metal.

words.meaning_en: to move toward the speaker or a specific place; to arrive; to happen or occur
words.meaning_es: venir; moverse hacia el hablante o un lugar específico; llegar; suceder u ocurrir
words.origin_en: From Old English 'cuman', from Proto-Germanic. One of the most basic and ancient verbs in English.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'cuman', del proto-germánico. Uno de los verbos más básicos y antiguos en inglés.
examples: Come here, please!
Can you come to my house tomorrow?
Spring is coming soon.
Come with me to the store.
The package should come today.
Dreams come true when you work hard.
Where do you come from?
Come on! Let's go!
Winter is coming.
words.note: ⚠️ Key difference: Use 'COME' for movement TOWARD the speaker or destination. Example: 'Come to my office' (you move toward me). Contrast with 'GO' which means movement AWAY.

words.meaning_en: to move away from the speaker or to a different place; to leave; to function or work
words.meaning_es: ir; moverse lejos del hablante o a un lugar diferente; salir; funcionar
words.origin_en: From Old English 'gān', from Proto-Germanic. Another fundamental verb expressing movement.
words.origin_es: Del inglés antiguo 'gān', del proto-germánico. Otro verbo fundamental que expresa movimiento.
examples: I have to go now.
Let's go to the park.
Where are you going?
Go away!
The car won't go. (doesn't work)
Time goes by so fast.
Go ahead and start without me.
How did the meeting go?
Go for it! (do it!)
I'm going home.
words.note: ⚠️ Key difference: Use 'GO' for movement AWAY from the speaker or toward another destination. Example: 'Go to his office' (you move away from me toward him). Contrast with 'COME' which means movement TOWARD.

words.meaning_en: a hard, aromatic seed from a tropical tree used as a spice in cooking and baking; in soccer, a move where you kick the ball between an opponent's legs
words.meaning_es: nuez moscada; semilla aromática de un árbol tropical usada como especia en cocina; en fútbol, un movimiento donde pasas el balón entre las piernas del oponente
words.origin_en: From Middle English 'notemuge', from Old French 'nois muguede', meaning 'musk-scented nut'. The tree is native to Indonesia.
words.origin_es: Del inglés medio 'notemuge', del francés antiguo 'nois muguede', que significa 'nuez con aroma a almizcle'. El árbol es nativo de Indonesia.
examples: Add a pinch of nutmeg to the pumpkin pie.
Nutmeg is commonly used in holiday recipes.
She grated fresh nutmeg over the eggnog.
The dish has a hint of nutmeg and cinnamon.
The player nutmegged the defender! (soccer move)
Nutmeg should be used sparingly as it has a strong flavor.

words.meaning_en: a medical device consisting of a tube and plunger used to inject fluids into or withdraw fluids from the body
words.meaning_es: jeringa; dispositivo médico que consiste en un tubo y émbolo usado para inyectar o extraer fluidos del cuerpo
words.origin_en: From Late Latin 'syringa', from Greek 'syrinx' meaning 'pipe' or 'tube'. Originally referred to any pipe-like instrument.
words.origin_es: Del latín tardío 'syringa', del griego 'syrinx' que significa 'tubo' o 'conducto'. Originalmente se refería a cualquier instrumento en forma de tubo.
examples: The nurse prepared the syringe for the injection.
He has a fear of needles and syringes.
The doctor used a syringe to draw blood.
Make sure the syringe is sterile before use.
She filled the syringe with the medication.
Dispose of used syringes in the sharps container.

words.meaning_en: to arrive or appear, especially when expected; to be visible or become noticeable; to embarrass someone by being better than them
words.meaning_es: aparecer o llegar, especialmente cuando se espera; ser visible o hacerse notorio; avergonzar a alguien siendo mejor que ellos
words.origin_en: Combination of 'show' (Old English 'sceawian' = to look at) and 'up' (Old English 'upp'). The phrasal verb emerged in American English in the late 1800s.
words.origin_es: Combinación de 'show' (inglés antiguo 'sceawian' = mirar) y 'up' (inglés antiguo 'upp'). El verbo frasal surgió en inglés americano a finales de los 1800s.
examples: He didn't show up for the meeting.
What time did you show up?
She always shows up late.
The stain will show up on white fabric.
He showed up his colleagues with his presentation.
I hope they show up on time.
Don't try to show me up in front of my boss!
The problem showed up after the update.

words.meaning_en: to stay in a place longer than necessary; to remain or persist; to take a long time to leave or disappear
words.meaning_es: permanecer; quedarse en un lugar más tiempo del necesario; persistir; tardar mucho tiempo en irse o desaparecer
words.origin_en: From Middle English 'lengeren', meaning 'to dwell, tarry'. Related to Old English 'lengan' (to prolong).
words.origin_es: Del inglés medio 'lengeren', que significa 'permanecer, demorar'. Relacionado con el inglés antiguo 'lengan' (prolongar).
examples: Don't linger in the hallway.
The smell of coffee lingered in the air.
She lingered at the door, hoping to talk to him.
The pain lingered for several days.
They lingered over dinner, enjoying the conversation.
Doubts still linger in my mind.
The fog lingered until noon.
He lingered behind after class to ask a question.

words.meaning_en: a tool with a rubber cup on a stick used to clear blocked drains and toilets; the part of a syringe or pump that moves up and down
words.meaning_es: desatascador; herramienta con una ventosa de goma en un palo usada para desatascar desagües e inodoros; el émbolo de una jeringa o bomba que se mueve arriba y abajo
words.origin_en: From 'plunge' (to thrust into water or downward) + '-er' (agent suffix). The verb 'plunge' comes from Old French 'plongier'.
words.origin_es: De 'plunge' (sumergir o empujar hacia abajo) + '-er' (sufijo de agente). El verbo 'plunge' viene del francés antiguo 'plongier'.
examples: I need a plunger to unclog the toilet.
Use the plunger to clear the drain.
The plunger of the syringe was stuck.
She bought a new plunger at the hardware store.
Push down on the plunger firmly.
The plunger creates suction to remove blockages.

words.meaning_en: the highest point or top of something; in geometry, a point where two or more lines meet to form an angle; the top of the head; plural: vertices
words.meaning_es: vértice; el punto más alto o la cima de algo; en geometría, un punto donde dos o más líneas se encuentran para formar un ángulo; la parte superior de la cabeza
words.origin_en: From Latin 'vertex' meaning 'highest point, top', from 'vertere' (to turn). Used in mathematics since the 1500s.
words.origin_es: Del latín 'vertex' que significa 'punto más alto, cima', de 'vertere' (girar). Usado en matemáticas desde los 1500s.
examples: The vertex of the triangle is at the top.
Each vertex of the cube connects three edges.
The vertex of the angle measures 90 degrees.
The mountain's vertex was covered in snow.
In the graph, each vertex represents a node.
The vertices of the polygon must be connected.

words.meaning_en: custom-made or adapted to fit a particular person or purpose; specifically designed or modified to suit particular needs or requirements
words.meaning_es: hecho a medida; adaptado para ajustarse a una persona o propósito particular; diseñado específicamente para satisfacer necesidades particulares
words.origin_en: From 'tailor' (Middle English 'taillour', from Old French 'tailleur'), meaning one who cuts and fits clothes. The adjective form emerged in the 1800s.
words.origin_es: De 'tailor' (sastre en inglés medio 'taillour', del francés antiguo 'tailleur'), que significa quien corta y ajusta ropa. La forma adjetiva surgió en los 1800s.
examples: She wore a beautifully tailored suit.
The program is tailored to meet individual needs.
He received tailored training for his new role.
The course is tailored for beginners.
They offer tailored solutions for each client.
The jacket was perfectly tailored to fit him.

words.meaning_en: the activity of making things skillfully by hand; creating or designing something with care and skill; in video games, making items from materials
words.meaning_es: artesanía; la actividad de hacer cosas hábilmente a mano; crear o diseñar algo con cuidado y habilidad; en videojuegos, fabricar objetos con materiales
words.origin_en: From 'craft' (Old English 'cræft' meaning skill, strength, power). The '-ing' form emerged as crafts became hobbies and creative activities.
words.origin_es: De 'craft' (inglés antiguo 'cræft' que significa habilidad, fuerza, poder). La forma '-ing' surgió cuando las artesanías se volvieron pasatiempos y actividades creativas.
examples: She enjoys crafting handmade jewelry.
Crafting with paper is a fun activity for kids.
He's been crafting a new message for the presentation.
The game requires crafting weapons from raw materials.
Crafting the perfect resume takes time and effort.
They're crafting a solution to the problem.

words.meaning_en: the fact or condition of being responsible and answerable for one's actions, decisions, or policies; obligation to accept responsibility or to account for one's actions
words.meaning_es: responsabilidad; el hecho o condición de ser responsable y tener que responder por las acciones, decisiones o políticas propias; obligación de aceptar responsabilidad por las acciones
words.origin_en: From 'accountable' (from Old French 'acontable') + '-ity' (suffix meaning state or condition). Emerged in the late 1700s in political and business contexts.
words.origin_es: De 'accountable' (del francés antiguo 'acontable') + '-ity' (sufijo que significa estado o condición). Surgió a finales de los 1700s en contextos políticos y empresariales.
examples: The company promotes accountability at all levels.
Leaders must take accountability for their decisions.
We need more accountability in government.
Personal accountability is key to success.
The new system ensures greater accountability.
He accepted full accountability for the mistake.
Accountability builds trust in organizations.

words.meaning_en: causing or likely to cause harm, damage, or injury; detrimental or injurious to health, well-being, or the environment
words.meaning_es: dañino; que causa o puede causar daño, perjuicio o lesión; perjudicial para la salud, el bienestar o el medio ambiente
words.origin_en: From 'harm' (Old English 'hearm' meaning injury, evil, grief) + '-ful' (suffix meaning full of). Used since Middle English.
words.origin_es: De 'harm' (inglés antiguo 'hearm' que significa lesión, mal, pena) + '-ful' (sufijo que significa lleno de). Usado desde el inglés medio.
examples: Smoking is harmful to your health.
These chemicals are harmful to the environment.
Excessive screen time can be harmful to children.
The harmful effects of pollution are well documented.
Avoid exposure to harmful substances.
Spreading rumors can be harmful to relationships.
The harmful bacteria caused food poisoning.

words.meaning_en: 1. A person who plays bowling or cricket (player who throws the ball). 2. A bowler hat (a hard felt hat with a rounded crown).
words.meaning_es: 1. Una persona que juega boliche o cricket (jugador que lanza la pelota). 2. Un sombrero bombín (un sombrero duro de fieltro con corona redondeada).
words.origin_en: From 'bowl' (from Old French 'boule', from Latin 'bulla' meaning bubble or ball) + '-er' (agent suffix). The hat was named after London hat-makers Thomas and William Bowler in 1849.
words.origin_es: De 'bowl' (del francés antiguo 'boule', del latín 'bulla' que significa burbuja o bola) + '-er' (sufijo de agente). El sombrero fue nombrado en honor a los sombrereros londinenses Thomas y William Bowler en 1849.

words.meaning_en: 1. (Adjective/Informal) Feeling disappointed, sad, or upset about something. 2. (Verb - past tense of 'bum') To beg or obtain something from someone; to mooch.
words.meaning_es: 1. (Adjetivo/Informal) Sentirse decepcionado, triste o molesto por algo. 2. (Verbo - pasado de 'bum') Pedir prestado u obtener algo de alguien; gorronear.
words.origin_en: From 'bum' (1960s American slang meaning to feel depressed or disappointed), possibly derived from 'bummer' (a bad experience). The verb form 'bum' (meaning to beg or borrow) comes from 'bummer' (1855), a loafer or vagrant, from German 'bummler' (loafer).
words.origin_es: De 'bum' (jerga americana de los años 1960 que significa sentirse deprimido o decepcionado), posiblemente derivado de 'bummer' (una mala experiencia). La forma verbal 'bum' (que significa mendigar o pedir prestado) proviene de 'bummer' (1855), un holgazán o vagabundo, del alemán 'bummler' (holgazán).

words.meaning_en: 1. (Verb - past tense) Ran away suddenly and quickly, often in fear or panic. 2. Fastened or secured something with a bolt. 3. Ate food very quickly without chewing properly.
words.meaning_es: 1. (Verbo - pasado) Huyó repentinamente y rápidamente, a menudo por miedo o pánico. 2. Aseguró o fijó algo con un perno. 3. Comió comida muy rápidamente sin masticar bien.
words.origin_en: From 'bolt' (Old English 'bolt', meaning arrow or missile), extended to mean sudden movement like an arrow. The fastening sense comes from the crossbow bolt shape of early door fasteners (14th century). The eating sense emerged in the 19th century.
words.origin_es: De 'bolt' (inglés antiguo 'bolt', que significa flecha o proyectil), extendido para significar movimiento repentino como una flecha. El sentido de sujeción proviene de la forma de perno de ballesta de los primeros cierres de puertas (siglo XIV). El sentido de comer surgió en el siglo XIX.

words.meaning_en: 1. (Noun/Verb) Physical or mental pressure, stress, or tension. 2. To injure a muscle or body part by overexertion. 3. To make a great effort. 4. To filter or separate liquid from solid. 5. A breed, variety, or type.
words.meaning_es: 1. (Sustantivo/Verbo) Presión física o mental, estrés o tensión. 2. Lesionar un músculo o parte del cuerpo por esfuerzo excesivo. 3. Hacer un gran esfuerzo. 4. Filtrar o separar líquido de sólido. 5. Una raza, variedad o tipo.
words.origin_en: From Old French 'estreindre' (to press, squeeze tightly), from Latin 'stringere' (to draw tight, press together). Used since the 14th century for tension and effort, extended to biological varieties in the 19th century.
words.origin_es: Del francés antiguo 'estreindre' (presionar, apretar), del latín 'stringere' (apretar, presionar juntos). Usado desde el siglo XIV para tensión y esfuerzo, extendido a variedades biológicas en el siglo XIX.

words.meaning_en: In a steady manner; continuously, regularly, and gradually without interruption or sudden changes; firmly and reliably.
words.meaning_es: De manera constante; continua, regular y gradualmente sin interrupción o cambios repentinos; firme y confiablemente.
words.origin_en: From 'steady' (from Old English 'stede', meaning place or position, implying firmness) + '-ly' (adverb suffix). The word 'steady' evolved to mean stable and constant in the 16th century, with 'steadily' following as its adverb form.
words.origin_es: De 'steady' (del inglés antiguo 'stede', que significa lugar o posición, implicando firmeza) + '-ly' (sufijo adverbial). La palabra 'steady' evolucionó para significar estable y constante en el siglo XVI, con 'steadily' siguiendo como su forma adverbial.

words.meaning_en: 1. (Verb - past tense) Defended or protected oneself from attack or danger. 2. Looked after or provided for oneself without help (often used with 'for'). 3. Pushed away or warded off something.
words.meaning_es: 1. (Verbo - pasado) Se defendió o protegió de un ataque o peligro. 2. Se cuidó o proveyó para sí mismo sin ayuda (a menudo usado con 'for'). 3. Rechazó o alejó algo.
words.origin_en: From 'fend', shortened form of 'defend' (from Old French 'defendre', from Latin 'defendere' meaning to ward off). First used in the 13th century. The phrase 'fend for oneself' emerged in the 16th century.
words.origin_es: De 'fend', forma abreviada de 'defend' (del francés antiguo 'defendre', del latín 'defendere' que significa repeler). Usado por primera vez en el siglo XIII. La frase 'fend for oneself' surgió en el siglo XVI.

words.meaning_en: 1. (Verb/Noun) Exhales audibly to express sadness, relief, tiredness, or contentment. 2. Makes a sound similar to exhaling (like wind). 3. An act of sighing.
words.meaning_es: 1. (Verbo/Sustantivo) Exhalar audiblemente para expresar tristeza, alivio, cansancio o satisfacción. 2. Hacer un sonido similar a exhalar (como el viento). 3. Un acto de suspirar.
words.origin_en: From Middle English 'sighen', alteration of 'siken', probably from Old English 'sīcan' (to sigh). The word has been used to express emotion through breathing since the 13th century.
words.origin_es: Del inglés medio 'sighen', alteración de 'siken', probablemente del inglés antiguo 'sīcan' (suspirar). La palabra se ha usado para expresar emoción a través de la respiración desde el siglo XIII.

words.meaning_en: 1. (Verb/Noun) Crying out loudly in grief, pain, or distress with prolonged high-pitched sounds. 2. Making a long, high sound expressing sadness. 3. The act or sound of wailing.
words.meaning_es: 1. (Verbo/Sustantivo) Llorar en voz alta con pena, dolor o angustia con sonidos prolongados y agudos. 2. Hacer un sonido largo y agudo que expresa tristeza. 3. El acto o sonido de lamentarse.
words.origin_en: From Middle English 'wailen', probably from Old Norse 'væla' (to wail, lament). Related to Old English 'wā' (woe). The word has been used since the 14th century to describe loud lamentation.
words.origin_es: Del inglés medio 'wailen', probablemente del nórdico antiguo 'væla' (lamentarse). Relacionado con el inglés antiguo 'wā' (ay, dolor). La palabra se ha usado desde el siglo XIV para describir lamentaciones fuertes.

words.meaning_en: 1. (Noun) The space beside a bed, especially in relation to caring for a sick person. 2. (Adjective) Located next to a bed or relating to the area beside a bed.
words.meaning_es: 1. (Sustantivo) El espacio al lado de una cama, especialmente en relación con el cuidado de una persona enferma. 2. (Adjetivo) Ubicado junto a una cama o relacionado con el área al lado de una cama.
words.origin_en: Compound word from 'bed' (Old English 'bedd', meaning sleeping place) + 'side' (Old English 'sīde', meaning flank or edge). Used since the 16th century, especially in medical contexts referring to patient care.
words.origin_es: Palabra compuesta de 'bed' (inglés antiguo 'bedd', que significa lugar para dormir) + 'side' (inglés antiguo 'sīde', que significa costado o borde). Usado desde el siglo XVI, especialmente en contextos médicos refiriéndose al cuidado del paciente.
